rulings.Case Title: LIAN TONG, LLC VS. BING WU CONSTRUCTION COMPANY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion For Judgment On The Pleadins And/Or Motion To Strike Bing Wu Construction Company'S First Amended X-Complaint
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on the Law and Motion Calendar for Tuesday, May 14, 2019, line 2. PLAINTIFF LIAN TONG, LLC Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings And/Or Motion To Strike Bing Wu Construction Company'S First Amended X-Complaint. *** Tentative Ruling - Part 1 of 2 *** Plaintiff and Cross-Defendant Lian Tong LLC's motion for judgment on the pleadings is granted with 30 days leave to amend as to causes of action 3-6 and without leave to amend as to causes of action 1 and 2. The court recognizes that Lian Tong has yet to file an answer to the amended cross-complaint so this motion for judgment on the pleadings is technically premature. (See Code Civ. Proc. § 438(f)(2).) Nevertheless, Lian Tong properly notes several defects with the amended cross-complaint and the court will address the merits. The court will construe the motion for judgment on the pleadings as a demurrer and/or entertain the motion for judgment on the pleadings on its own motion. (See Code Civ. Proc. § 438(b)(2).) The motion for judgment on the pleadings is granted without leave to amend as to causes of action 1 and 2. The only cognizable damages that are alleged for breach of the 2015 cancellation agreement are attorney's fees and defense costs. Absent a contractual attorneys' fees clause, those damages are not recoverable as a matter of law. (See Navellier v. Sletten (2003) 106 Cal.App.4th 763, 777 [party could not recover attorney's fees as damages on claim for breach of release].) The motion for judgment on the pleadings is granted with leave to amend as to cause of action 6. As pled, that declaratory relief cause of action is merely another attempt to recover attorney's fees/defense costs. Bing Wu purports to seek declaratory relief as to other subjects, but does so only in the prayer for relief; accordingly, it is granted 30 days leave to amend the declaratory relief cause of action. Lian Tong's argument that the court should take judicial notice that Bing Wu "admitted" that the 2015 cancellation contract is not for rescission lacks merit as the terms and meaning of the contract are disputed and cannot be decided on the face of the pleadings. *** End of Part 1 - Tentative Ruling continues in Part 2 of 2 *** =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: LIAN TONG, LLC VS. BING WU CONSTRUCTION COMPANY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO STRIKE 1ST AMENDED CROSS COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on the Law and Motion Calendar for Tuesday, May 14, 2019, line 2. CROSS DEFENDANT LIAN TONG, LLC'S MOTION TO STRIKE 1ST AMENDED CROSS COMPLAINT. Plaintiff and Cross-Defendant Lian Tong LLC's motion to strike is granted as to the prayer for attorneys' fees and the prayer for punitive damages on the third cause of action for negligent misrepresentation. As discussed in the court's ruling on the motion for judgment on the pleadings, Defendant and Cross-Complainant Bing Wu has not alleged any contractual or statutory basis for recovering its attorney's fees. Punitive damages are not recoverable for negligent representation. (Alliance Mortgage Co. v. Rothwell (1995) 10 Cal.4th 1226, 1241.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: LIAN TONG, LLC VS. BING WU CONSTRUCTION COMPANY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiff Lian Tong, Llc Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings And/Or Motion To Strike Bing Wu Construction Company'S First Amended X-Complaint (Added For Purposes Of Entering Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on the Law and Motion Calendar for Tuesday, May 14, 2019, line 2. Plaintiff Lian Tong, Llc Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings And/Or Motion To Strike Bing Wu Construction Company'S First Amended X-Complaint. *** Tentative Ruling - Part 2 of 2 *** The motion for judgment on the pleadings is granted with 30 days leave to amend as to causes of action 3-5. The parties cannot agree on what is actually pleaded in this case. Lian Tong argues that all of the claims are predicated on the 2015 cancellation agreement. Bing Wu claims that its claims are based both on the 2014 construction agreement and the 2015 cancellation agreement. Bing Wu has leave to amend to segregate its distinct fraud claims per California Rule of Court 2.112. Causes of action 3 -5 each currently plead two theories of liability that should be separated. Paragraphs 49, 58, and 67 allege that (1) Bing Wu was duped into entering into the 2014 construction contract because Lian Tong misrepresented that it had all of the required permits/approvals; and (2) Lian Tong is acting fraudulently because in the 2015 cancellation agreement it agreed to release Bing Wu but then in violation of that agreement Lian Tong filed this action. Those are two distinct theories of liability that should be separated. With regard to the fraud claims based on the 2015 cancellation agreement, Bing Wu has leave to plead those claims to allege damages other than attorney's fees. The tort of another doctrine does not apply and attorney's fees are generally not recoverable as fraud damages. (See Bezaire v. Fidelity & Deposit Co. (1970) 12 Cal.App.3d 888, 892.) The court rejects Lian Tong's argument that the litigation privilege categorically bars the entire amended cross-complaint. The litigation privilege can apply to breach of contract claims. "[W]hether the litigation privilege applies to an action for breach of contract turns on whether its application furthers the policies underlying the privilege." (Wentland v. Wass (2005) 126 Cal.App.4th 1484, 1492; see also Vivian v. Labrucherie (2013) 214 Cal.App.4th 267, 276.) Here, Bing Wu basically alleges that the parties entered into a covenant not to sue. Such an agreement does not implicate the litigation privilege. (See Navellier v. Sletten (2003) 106 Cal.App.4th 763, 774.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. *** End of Tentative Ruling *** =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: STACEY CURTIS VS. HOMEBRIDGE, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Arbitration
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on the Law and Motion Calendar for Tuesday, May 14, 2019, line 5. DEFENDANT HOMEBRIDGE, INC.'S Motion To Compel Arbitration. *** Tentative Ruling - Part 1 of 2 *** Defendant Homebridge, Inc.'s motion to compel arbitration is granted as to Plaintiff Stacey Curtis' fifth cause of action for wrongful termination in violation of public policy and denied as to her second, third, and fourth causes of action for retaliation, harassment, and failure to prevent harassment under the Fair Employment and Housing Act (FEHA). Plaintiff's FEHA claims are not arbitrable as those claims are statutory. "Generally, a collective bargaining agreement (CBA) providing for arbitration of employment grievances does not provide for arbitration of a worker's claims based on violations of state anti-discrimination or retaliation statutes, nor do federal labor relations laws preempt such claims." (Rymel v. Save Mart Supermarkets, Inc. (2018) 30 Cal.App.5th 853, 856.) Judge Kahn's March 26, 2018 Order denied a prior motion to compel arbitration of Labor Code claims based on the holding of Vasserman v. Henry Mayo Newhall Memorial Hospital (2017) 8 Cal.App.5th 236 that "the presumption of arbitrability that typically applies to contractual disputes arising out of a CBA is not applicable" where a party asserts statutory violations. In such circumstances, the CBA must "contain a clear and unmistakable waiver of the covered employees' rights to a judicial forum relating to the statutory claims alleged in the complaint." (Id. at 245 [quotations omitted].) Mendez v. Mid-Wilshire Health Care Center (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 534 and Vasquez v. Superior Court (2000) 80 Cal.App.4th 430 applied the same principle in affirming denials of motions to compel arbitration of FEHA claims. Both concluded that broad arbitration clause language without an explicit incorporation of specific statutory requirements was not enough to find the claims were arbitrable. (See Mendez v. Mid-Wilshire Health Care Center (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 534, 543-46 [holding that broad arbitration clause combined with general antidiscrimination provisions that did not mention FEHA specifically was not enough to waive the plaintiff's right to litigate FEHA claims]; Vasquez v. Superior Court (2000) 80 Cal.App.4th 430 ["[T]here is no express provision that the antidiscrimination commitment is subject to the grievance and arbitration provisions. Nor are the ADA and the FEHA explicitly incorporated in the CBA. Indeed, these statutes are not even mentioned."].) Here, as in Mendez and Vasquez, the CBA's broad arbitration clause covering disputes under the agreement and a general "No Discrimination" section that does not explicitly mention FEHA do not clearly and unmistakably waive Plaintiff's right to bring her statutory claims in court. (Pitchford Decl. Exh. F, p. 3, 7.) Furthermore, even if an employee initiates the CBA grievance process leading to arbitration, that employee is not required to exhaust the process by going through binding arbitration unless the contract explicitly states the grievance procedure applies to the employee's claims. (Ahmadi-Kashani v. Regents of Univ. of Cal. (2008) 159 Cal.App.4th 449, 456-60.) The CBA's arbitration clause does not explicitly cover the retaliation and harassment claims and the arbitration provision merely says that the parties "may" arbitrate unresolved grievances. (Pitchford Decl. Exh. F, p. 7.) Thus, Plaintiff's FEHA claims are not arbitrable. *** END OF PART 1 - TENTATIVE RULING CONTINUES IN PART 2 OF 2 *** =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: STACEY CURTIS VS. HOMEBRIDGE, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Homebridge, Inc.'S Motion To Compel Arbitration. (Added For Purposes Of Entering Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on the Law and Motion Calendar for Tuesday, May 14, 2019, line 5. Defendant Homebridge, Inc.'S Motion To Compel Arbitration. *** Tentative Ruling Part 2 of 2 *** Plaintiff's fifth cause of action for wrongful termination in violation of public policy is not statutory and therefore is covered by the CBA's arbitration provision. The CBA applies to Plaintiff because she signed the Employee Handbook that references the CBA and because union-negotiated contracts may properly cover employees who are not formally parties to the contract. (See Florio v. City of Ontario (2005) 130 Cal.App.4th 1462, 1466 ["[A] member of a bargaining unit is bound by the terms of a valid collective bargaining agreement, though he is not formally a party to it and may not even belong to the union which negotiated it."].) Defendant did not waive its right to arbitration because the wrongful termination claim was not asserted until the second amended complaint was filed in March 2019, only two months ago. The arbitration agreement in the CBA is not unenforceable as unconscionable. It suffers from only a modest degree of procedural unconscionability. The Employee Handbook mentions the CBA, but does not refer to arbitration. In addition, the arbitration provision is found on page 7 of the CBA in Section 16, which is titled "Grievance Procedure," and does not mention arbitration until Step 4. The punitive damages waiver is substantively unconscionable. (Armendariz v. Foundation Health Psychcare Services, Inc. (2000) 24 Cal.4th 83, 103-04; Pitchford Decl. Exh. F, p. 8.) However, this unconscionability does not permeate the entire contract and so the Court severs this provision. Also in accordance with Armendariz, the costs borne by Plaintiff in arbitration cannot exceed the costs of pursuing this claim in court and the cost-sharing provision in the CBA is inapplicable to the extent it imposes additional burden on Plaintiff. (Armendariz, 24 Cal.4th at 113; Pitchford Decl. Exh. F, p. 7.) Finally, under the Federal Arbitration Act and the California Arbitration Act, the Court must stay the remaining civil action claims in this court while the wrongful termination claim is arbitrated. (9 U.S.C. § 3; Code Civ. Proc. § 1281.4.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the Defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. *** End of Tenative Ruling *** =(302/EPS)

Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Hearing required. Start time for hearing is 1:30 pm. rulings.Case Title: MARIA ELENA MELGOZA ET AL VS. SHREE JALARAM MOTEL, L.P. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO SET ASIDE DEFAULT/DEFAULT JUDGMENT/LEAVE TO DEFEND
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on the Law and Motion Calendar for Tuesday, May 14, 2019, line 6. DEFENDANT SHREE SHIVA MURUGA, L.P.'S MOTION TO SET ASIDE DEFAULT/DEFAULT JUDGMENT/LEAVE TO DEFEND. Defendant Shree Shiva Muruga, L.P.'s motion to vacate the default entered on February 7, 2019 is granted. The motion is timely as it was filed approximately two months after the default was entered. The defendant provides a proposed pleading. Defendant demonstrates excusable neglect, mistake, and/or inadvertence caused entry of the default. Defendant's counsel was experiencing family issues during the time defendant's response was due. "[T]he policy of the law is to have every litigated case tried upon its merits, and it looks with disfavor upon a party, who, regardless of the merits of the case, attempts to take advantage of the mistake, surprise, inadvertence, or neglect of his adversary." (Weitz v. Yankosky (1966) 63 Cal.2d 849, 855; see also Fasuyi v. Permatex, Inc. (2008) 167 Cal.App.4th 681, 701.) Defendant shall file its response by May 21, 2019. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the Defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: FAST TRACK INVESTMENT COMPANY LLC A DELAWARE VS. LESLEY REED ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO STRIKE 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on the Law and Motion Calendar for Tuesday, May 14, 2019, line 7. DEFENDANT JOHN FITZPATRICK VANNUCCI'S MOTION TO STRIKE 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT. Defendant John Fitzpatrick Vannucci's motion to strike portions of plaintiff Fast Track Investment Company LLC's first amended complaint is denied as to the prayer for compensatory damages and is granted without leave to amend as to the prayer for attorney fees. As the court previously ruled in denying Mr. Vanucci's motion to strike the prayer for relief in the original complaint, the amount of compensatory damages is adequately alleged (FAC 21) and Fast Trak need not plead evidentiary facts to support those allegations. The prayer for attorney fees against Mr. Vanucci is not supported by the facts alleged on the face of the complaint which include the matters shown in exhibits attached to the complaint. Mr. Vanucci is not a signatory to the Agreement between Fast Trak and Ms. Reed, and the Acknowledgment signed by Mr. Vanucci does not have an attorney fee provision and does not incorporate by reference the attorney fee provision from the Agreement. (See Barnett v. Fireman's Fund Ins. Co. (2001) 90 Cal.App.4th 500, 505 ["to the extent the factual allegations conflict with the content of the exhibits to the complaint, we rely on and accept as true the contents of the exhibits and treat as surplusage the pleader's allegations as to the legal effect of the exhibits."]. The reciprocity language of Civ. Code § 1717 (a) does not support Fast Trak's contention that it can enforce a contractual attorney fee provision against a non-party to the contract. Section 1717 safeguards mutuality of remedy in two circumstances, neither of which applies here. (See California-American Water Co. v. Marina Coast Water Dist. (2017) 18 Cal.App.5th 571, 577.) Berge v. International Harvester Co. (1983) 142 Cal.App.3d 152 is distinguishable, because there the plaintiff and cross-defendant was a party to a retail sales contract containing an attorney's fees clause and her opponent was the assignee of the contract. Because Fast Trak has not shown that further amendment is capable of curing the deficiency in its pleading, the motion to strike is granted without leave to amend. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the Defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: KERI FINDLEY VS. CORE VENTURE CAPITAL LLC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Core Venture Capital Llc, Kathleen Utecht Notice Of Motion And Motion For A Protective Order And Sanctions;
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on the Law and Motion Calendar for Tuesday, May 14, 2019, line 8. Defendants Core Venture Capital LLC, Kathleen Utecht's Motion For A Protective Order And Sanctions. The court adopts the report and recommendation of the judge pro tem. Defendants' motion for a protective order is granted. The parties shall sign and submit for entry by the court Defendants' proposed protective order, subject to the following conditions. Plaintiff's request for an order allow her to view materials designated as Attorneys' Eyes Only is denied, without prejudice to her ability to challenge any such designations as overbroad. The protective order shall contain a provision allowing Plaintiff to use documents produced in this action in her divorce proceeding with Peter Findley, provided that Plaintiff gives Defendants advance notice and an opportunity to object before introducing any documents from this action in that proceeding and that she ensures that any such documents are subject to the same level of protection as that granted by the proposed protective order in this action. Defendants' motion for sanctions is denied. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: ANDREW HOYEM VS. WILLIAM BARLOW ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Strike Plaintiffs First Amended Complaint
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on the Law and Motion Calendar for Tuesday, May 14, 2019, line 9. DEFENDANTS WILLIAM BARLOW, LESLIE BERRIMAN, PHILLIP BOWLES, WILLIAM BUICE, KEVIN KING, HELYN MACLEAN, WILHELM OEHL, PAUL WATTIS, GRABHORN INSTITUTE, LYRA CORPORATION'S Motion To Strike Plaintiffs First Amended Complaint. *** Tentative Ruling - Part 1 of 2 *** Defendants Grabhorn Institute, Lyra Corporation, William Barlow, Leslie Berriman, Phillip Bowles, William Buice, Kevin King, Helyn Maclean, Wilhelm Oehl and Paul Wattis' motion to strike portions of the first amended complaint is denied. The notice of motion is improperly directed at portions of Plaintiff's original complaint, which has been superseded by the first amended complaint. Defendants' various arguments lack merit. Defendants' contention that punitive damages are unavailable because this action is "really based on the alleged breach of contract in the promissory notes" (Dem. at 5) is groundless, because a promissory note or other written contract may be an instrument by which a defendant engages in "financial abuse" of an elder within the meaning of the Elder Abuse Act. The Act specifically provides that financial abuse may be effected "by means of an agreement." (Welf. & Inst. Code § 15610.30(a)1),(c); see, e.g., Mahan v. Charles W. Chan Ins. Agency, Inc. (2017) 14 Cal.App.5th 841, 861-862 [complaint that alleged defendants arranged for restructuring of life insurance policies that resulted in more limited coverage at greater cost adequately alleged deprivation of property under Act].) Plaintiff's allegations that Defendants induced him to make multiple cash advances to them between September 28, 2010 and December 13, 2007, with the knowledge that those advances represented the only monies he had to fund his retirement and that they were unlikely to be repaid (FAC 32-34) are sufficient to allege that Defendants acted with recklessness, oppression, fraud, or malice. Defendants assert that Welf. & Inst. Code § 15657.5 applies only to conduct occurring after its effective date, but the first amended complaint expressly disclaims any liability on Defendants' part predating September 28, 2010, when Plaintiff allegedly made the first cash advance. (FAC 25.) Defendants' argument "There Is No Elder Abuse Act Claim Available to Hoyem As A Matter Of Law" is misplaced, as Defendants have not demurred to the second cause of action, and in any event again is improperly based on extrinsic matters outside the proper scope of demurrer. *** End of Part 1 - Tentative Ruling Continues in Part 2 of 2 *** =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: ANDREW HOYEM VS. WILLIAM BARLOW ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendants William Barlow, Leslie Berriman, Phillip Bowles, William Buice, Kevin King, Helyn Maclean, Wilhelm Oehl, Paul Wattis, Grabhorn Institute, Lyra Corporation'S Motion To Strike Plaintiffs First Amended Complaint (Added For Purposes Of Entering Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on the Law and Motion Calendar for Tuesday, May 14, 2019, line 9. Defendants William Barlow, Leslie Berriman, Phillip Bowles, William Buice, Kevin King, Helyn Maclean, Wilhelm Oehl, Paul Wattis, Grabhorn Institute, Lyra Corporation'S Motion To Strike Plaintiffs First Amended Complaint. *** Tentative Ruling - Part 2 of 2 *** Defendants' motion to strike the allegations that he was "threatened by Defendants with a LYRA bankruptcy" in which, as a result of how the promissory notes were structured, he would be relegated to the status of an equity security holder and likely would not be entitled to any distributions (FAC 28, 31, 34) is denied. Those allegations are relevant to Plaintiff's Elder Abuse Act claim, which requires a showing that Defendants deprived Plaintiff of a property interest for a wrongful use or with intent to defraud, and for that reason are not barred by the litigation privilege. (See Oren Royal Oaks Venture v. Greenberg, Bernhard, Weiss & Karma, Inc. (1988) 42 Cal.3d 1157, 1168 ["when allegations of misconduct properly put an individual's intent at issue in a civil action, statements made during the course of a judicial proceeding may be used for evidentiary purposes in determining whether the individual acted with the requisite intent."].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. *** End of Tentative Ruling *** =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: ANDREW HOYEM VS. WILLIAM BARLOW ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on the Law and Motion Calendar for Tuesday, May 14, 2019, line 9. DEFENDANTS WILLIAM BARLOW, LESLIE BERRIMAN, PHILLIP BOWLES, WILLIAM BUICE, KEVIN KING, HELYN MACLEAN, WILHELM OEHL, PAUL WATTIS, GRABHORN INSTITUTE, LYRA CORPORATION'S DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT. Defendants Grabhorn Institute, Lyra Corporation, William Barlow, Leslie Berriman, Phillip Bowles, William Buice, Kevin King, Helyn Maclean, Wilhelm Oehl and Paul Wattis' demurrer to the first cause of action of the first amended complaint for money and received and the third cause of action for declaratory relief re alter ego/joint agency is overruled. Defendants' extensive reliance on purported facts not alleged in the first amended complaint is improper on demurrer and cannot render the first amended complaint uncertain or defective. It is elementary that a demurrer can be used only to challenge defects that appear on the face of the pleading under attack or from matters outside the pleading that are judicially noticeable. (See generally California Practice Guide: Civil Procedure Before Trial (The Rutter Guide 2018) 7:8.) The court denies Defendants' request for judicial notice, which seeks notice of matters that are not properly the subject of judicial notice. The court will disregard the Roberts declaration and attached exhibits, which are improper for the same reason. The first cause of action, in conjunction with the alter ego and agency allegations (FAC 22-23), properly pleads a cause of action for money had and received. (FAC 22-23, 32, 36-41.) "A cause of action for money had and received is stated if it is alleged that the defendant is indebted to the plaintiff in a certain sum for money had and received by the defendant for the use of the plaintiff. The claim is viable wherever one person has received money which belongs to another, and which in equity and good conscience should be paid over to the latter." (Avidor v. Sutter's Place, Inc. (2013) 212 Cal.App.4th 1439, 1454 (citations and internal quotations omitted).) Defendants' arguments raise factual issues that are not properly considered on demurrer. The third cause of action adequately pleads a cause of action for declaratory relief regarding whether Defendants are alter egos or agents of each other. A general demurrer to a cause of action for declaratory relief must be overruled so long as an actual controversy is alleged; the pleader need not establish it is also entitled to a favorable judgment. (Ludgate Ins. Co. v. Lockheed Martin Corp. (2000) 82 Cal.App.4th 592, 606; see also Qualified Patients Ass'n v. City of Anaheim (2010) 187 Cal.App.4th 734, 751 [" '[a] general demurrer is usually not an appropriate method for testing the merits of a declaratory relief action . . . .'"].) Contrary to Defendants' contention, a plaintiff alleging an alter ego theory is required to allege only ultimate rather than evidentiary facts. (Rutherford Holdings, LLC v. Plaza Del Rey (2014) 223 Cal.App.4th 221, 235-236 [holding that plaintiff made sufficient allegations of alter ego to avoid a demurrer].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)
